Home › Density Profiles of Dark Matter

Density Profiles of Dark Matter

A Density Profile (\( ho(r) \)) describes how the concentration of matter varies with the distance from the center of a system. In university-level cosmology, determining the density profile of Dark Matter Halos is the primary way to test different candidates for Dark Matter. It bridges the gap between the microscopic properties of particles (like WIMPs or Axions) and the macroscopic 4D structure of galaxies.

1. The NFW Profile: The Standard Model

Large-scale N-body simulations of Cold Dark Matter (CDM) consistently produce the Navarro-Frenk-White (NFW) Profile: \[ ho(r) = \frac{ ho_s}{\frac{r}{r_s}(1 + \frac{r}{r_s})^2} \] This profile features a "Cusp" at the center (\( ho \propto 1/r \)) and a steeper decay at large radii (\( ho \propto 1/r^3 \)). The NFW profile is a "Universal" result, appearing in everything from small subhalos to giant 1000-galaxy clusters.

2. The Cusp-Core Problem

The "Cusp" predicted by CDM is one of the greatest Theoretical Crises in the field. Observations of Galactic Rotation Curves in dwarf galaxies often show a "Core"—a flat central density profile (\( ho \approx ext{const} \)). This mismatch suggests that either dark matter is not purely collisionless (Self-Interacting Dark Matter) or that Astrophysical Dynamics (like gas bursts) "puff out" the center of the halo.

3. Einasto and Burkert Profiles

Physicists use alternative profiles to model specific systems:

  • Einasto Profile: A more flexible, non-power-law shape that often fits high-resolution simulations better than NFW.
  • Burkert Profile: A phenomenological core-profile used to represent galaxies where no cusp is seen, serving as the baseline for Modified Newtonian Dynamics (MOND) comparisons.

4. Gravitational Lensing Inversion

Density profiles are mapped directly using Gravitational Lensing. By observing the "Arcs" of light from background galaxies, astronomers can "invert" the distortion to find the total 4D mass distribution. This provides the most unbiased measure of the profile, proving that the Bullet Cluster and other mergers have profiles that are physically separated from the visible baryons.

5. Profiles in Self-Interacting Dark Matter (SIDM)

If dark matter particles can scatter off each other, the Total Dynamics of the halo center change. Frequent collisions transfer energy inward, "flattening" the NFW cusp into a core. Measuring the central density of Dark Matter Halos is therefore a direct probe of the Standard Model Conflict, potentially revealing a new subatomic force in the dark sector.

6. Cosmological Convergence

On the largest scales, the density profile of the entire universe is governed by the Scale Factor. The Cosmological Density of matter drops as \( a^{-3} \), while the Cosmological Constant remains uniform. The interaction between these different "global profiles" is what determines the Expansion History and the ultimate Big Freeze of the .

Key Theoretical Identities

Interact with identities to explore their semantic depth.

  • Density Spike [ Click to Expand Depth ]
    \[ ho(r) \propto r^{-\gamma} \quad ( ext{Power Law Base}) \]

    1. Physical Interpretation

    Density Spike: The sharp increase in dark matter concentration at the very heart of a galaxy.

    2. Symmetry & Origin

    Great Expansion: Symmetry derivation pending.

    3. Limits & Boundary

    Great Expansion: Limiting case analysis pending.

  • NFW Identity [ Click to Expand Depth ]
    \[ ho_{Einasto}(r) = ho_s \exp(-2n[(r/r_s)^{1/n} - 1]) \]

    1. Physical Interpretation

    NFW Identity: Why almost all dark matter clouds in simulations look exactly the same.

    2. Symmetry & Origin

    Great Expansion: Symmetry derivation pending.

    3. Limits & Boundary

    Great Expansion: Limiting case analysis pending.

  • Observation Gap [ Click to Expand Depth ]
    \[ M(r) = \int 4\pi r^2 ho(r) dr \]

    1. Physical Interpretation

    Observation Gap: The mystery of why real galaxies look "flatter" in the middle than math predicts.

    2. Symmetry & Origin

    Great Expansion: Symmetry derivation pending.

    3. Limits & Boundary

    Great Expansion: Limiting case analysis pending.

  • Mass Scan [ Click to Expand Depth ]
    \[ \frac{d\Phi}{dr} = \frac{G M(r)}{r^2} \quad ( ext{Potential link}) \]

    1. Physical Interpretation

    Mass Scan: Using the bend of light to draw a 3D map of how dark matter is packed.

    2. Symmetry & Origin

    Great Expansion: Symmetry derivation pending.

    3. Limits & Boundary

    Great Expansion: Limiting case analysis pending.
